karpelesappalachianEveryNight.title: Every Night When the Sun Goes In
karpelesappalachianEveryNight.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
Every night when the sun goes in,
Every night when the sun goes in,
I hang down my head and mournful cry.

True love, don't weep, true love, don't mourn,
True love, don't weep nor mourn for me,
I'm going away to Marble town.

I wish to the Lord that train would come
To take me back where I come from.

It's once my apron hung down low,
He'd follow me through both sleet and snow.

It's now my apron's to my chin,
He'll face my door and won't come in.

I wish to the Lord my babe was born,
A-sitting upon his pappy's knee,
And me, poor girl, was dead and gone,
And the green grass growing over me.

karpelesappalachianChickens.title: The Chickens They Are Crowing
karpelesappalachianChickens.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
The chickens they are crowing, a-crowing, a-crowing,
The chickens they are crowing, for it is almost daylight.

My mother she will scold me, will scold me, will scold me,
My mother she will scold me for staying away all night.

My father he'll uphold me, uphold me, uphold me,
My father he'll uphold me and say I'd a-done just right.

I won't go home till morning, till morning, till morning,
I won't go home till morning, and I'll stay with the girls all night.

The chickens they are crowing, a-crowing, a-crowing,
The chickens they are crowing, for it is almost daylight.

karpelesappalachianBrightMorningStar.title: Bright Morning Star
karpelesappalachianBrightMorningStar.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

Oh, where are our dear fathers?
Oh, where are our dear fathers?
Oh, where are our dear fathers?
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

They are down in the valley praying,
They are down in the valley praying,
They are down in the valley praying,
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

Oh, where are our dear mothers?
Oh, where are our dear mothers?
Oh, where are our dear mothers?
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

Some have gone to heaven shouting,
Some have gone to heaven shouting,
Some have gone to heaven shouting,
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Bright morning stars are rising,
Day is a-breaking in my soul.

karpelesappalachianOneMorningInMay.title: One Morning in May
karpelesappalachianOneMorningInMay.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
One morning in May, as I carelessly did stray,
I met a bold grenadier on my way.

"Good morning, good morning, good morning," said he,
"Oh, where are you going, my pretty lady?"

"I am going to the banks of the sea,
To see the waters gliding, hear the nightingale sing."

They hadn't been standing but a minute or two
When out of his knapsack a fiddle he drew.

And the tune that he played made the valleys ring,
Made the waters go gliding, made the nightingale sing.

"Bold soldier, kind soldier, will you marry me?"
"Oh, no, pretty lady, that never can be.

I've a wife in the army in my own country,
Two children beside her, and she would not like me."

"I'll go back to the army and stay there a year,
And often I'll think of you, my little dear.

And if ever I return, it will be in the spring,
And we'll both sit down together, hear the nightingale sing."

karpelesappalachianLittleMusgrave.title: Little Musgrave and Lady Barnard
karpelesappalachianLittleMusgrave.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
One day, one day, one holiday,
The first one of the year,
Little Matty Groves went to the church
The holy word to hear.

Lord Darnell's wife was standing by,
She cast her eye on him,
"Go home with me, little Matty Groves,
A wedded wife to be."

"I can't come home, and I won't come home,
I cannot spare my life,
But by the rings upon your hands
You are Lord Darnell's wife."

"It's if I am Lord Darnell's wife,
Lord Darnell's gone today,
He's across the waterside,
He's gone over there to stay."

But the little foot-page was standing by
And hearing what was said,
He swore Lord Darnell he would know
Before the sun was set.

He ran till he came to the riverside,
He bent his breast and swam,
He swam till he came to the other side
And he picked up his heels and ran.

"Oh, no your bower's not falling down,
Or does your castle burn,
But little Matty Groves he sleeps tonight
Keeping your lady warm."

Then Lord Darnell took his men
And lined them in a row;
The orders that he gave to them
That ne'er a horn should blow.

Matty Groves he was laid down,
He took a little sleep;
When he woke up Lord Darnell
Was standing at his feet.

"Rise up, rise up, little Matty Groves,
Rise up as quick as you can;
It shalln't be said in old Scotland
I slew a naked man."

"I've got two swords by my side,
They cost me from my purse;
You can have the very best
And I shall have the worst."

The very first stroke that Matty did give
He wounded Lord Darnell sore;
The very next stroke Lord Darnell gave
A word he spoke no more.

Then Lord Darnell took his wife
And set her on his knee;
"Who do you like best of all,
Little Matty Groves or me?"

"Very well I like your rosy cheeks,
Very well I like your chin,
But better I like little Matty Groves
Than you and all your kin."

karpelesappalachianFairMargaret.title: Fair Margaret and Sweet William
karpelesappalachianFairMargaret.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
Sweet William rose one morning bright
And dressed himself in blue,
"Come tell me now, you pretty little page,
What love there is 'twixt Lady Margaret and you."

"I know no harm of Lady Margaret,
And I hope she knows none of me;
But tomorrow morning at eight o'clock
Lady Margaret my bride shall be."

Lady Margaret sat in her chamber high
A-combing back her hair;
She spied Sweet William and his new bride
As they drew near to her.

Oh, down she laid her ivory comb
And back she tossed her hair,
And from her room a fair lady came
That was seen in there no more.

The day being gone and the night being come,
When most men were asleep,
Sweet William spied Lady Margaret's ghost
A-standing at his bed feet.

"How do you like your bed?" she said,
"And how do you like your sheet?
And how do you like that fair young bride
That lies in your arms asleep?"

"Full well I like my bed," he said,
"Full well I like my sheet,
But better I like that fair young maid
That stands at my bed feet."

He called up his merry men all,
By one, by two, by three;
"Go fetch me down Lady Margaret's corpse
For tomorrow she'll bury me."

He kissed her on her lily-white cheek,
He kissed her on her chin;
He kissed her on her clay-cold lips,
But never a smile came in.

Lady Margaret she died on the self-same day,
Sweet William he died the morrow;
Lady Margaret she died for love so true,
Sweet William he died for sorrow.

They buried her in the lower churchyard,
And him in the higher;
And out of her grave there grew a red rose,
And out of his a briar.

They grew and they grew to the church tower top
Until they could grow no higher,
And then they tied in a true lover's knot,
The red rose around the briar.

karpelesappalachianCruelShipsCarpenter.title: The Cruel Ship's Carpenter
karpelesappalachianCruelShipsCarpenter.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
Early one morning before the break of day,
He went to his Polly, these words he did say:
"O Polly, O Polly, you must go with me,
Before we are married my friends for to see."

He led her through groves and through valleys so deep,
Which caused pretty Polly to sigh and to weep;
"O Willie, O Willie, you've led me astray,
On purpose my innocent life to betray."

"It's true, it's true, those words you have said,
For all of this night long I've been digging your grave."
The grave being open and she standing by,
Which caused this poor maiden to sigh and to cry.

"O pardon, O pardon, O pardon," she cried,
"And I'll freely forgive you for the rest of my life."
"No pardon, no pardon, no pardon," said he,
"And into the grave her fair body threw."

He covered her up and he hastened away,
He left nothing behind but the wild birds to mourn.

karpelesappalachianGeordie.title: Geordie
karpelesappalachianGeordie.version: Cecil J. Sharp and Maud Karpeles, English Folk Songs from the Southern Appalachians (Oxford University Press, 1932)
stanzas:
As I walked out over London Bridge,
One misty morning early,
I overheard a fair, pretty maid
Was lamenting for her Geordie.

"My Geordie will be hanged in a golden chain,
'Tis not the chain of many;
He was born of a king's royal blood
And was loved by a virtuous lady."

"Go saddle me my milk-white steed,
Go saddle me my pony,
That I may ride to London's court
To plead for the life of Geordie."

"My Geordie never stole cow nor calf,
He never hurted any;
He stole sixteen of the king's royal deer
And he sold them in Bohenny."

"Six pretty babies have I born,
The seventh lies in my body;
I'd freely part with them every one
If you'd spare me the life of Geordie."

The judge looked over his left shoulder,
He said, "Fair maid, I'm sorry;
Fair maid, you must be gone,
For I cannot pardon Geordie."

"Let Geordie hang in a golden chain,
'Tis not the chain of many;
He was born of a king's royal blood
And was loved by a virtuous lady."
